title: Motivic Action Conjecture for Doi-Naganuma Lifts
speaker: Yingkun Li
abstract: A surprising property of the cohomology of locally symmetric spaces is that Hecke operators can act on multiple cohomological degrees with the same eigenvalues. In a series of papers, Venkatesh and his collaborators proposed an arithmetic reason for this: a hidden degree-shifting action of a certain motivic cohomology group. In this talk, we will explain the setup of this conjecture for coherent cohomology of Hilbert modular forms over ℂ, and give some recent result supporting it. This is joint work with Alex Horawa.

proof_stages.label: Classical modular-curve model for the action
proof_stages.summary: The speaker analyzes weight-one modular forms on a modular curve via coherent cohomology, Serre duality, Hecke eigenspaces, Petersson pairings, and Stark units, showing in this familiar case how a cohomological degree-shifting map can be normalized over \(\overline{\mathbb Q}\).

proof_stages.label: Derived-Hecke and motivic-action philosophy
proof_stages.summary: The recurring appearance of the same Hecke eigenvalues in several cohomological degrees is framed as the phenomenon that Venkatesh’s derived Hecke or motivic action is meant to explain by producing degree-raising operations.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular motivic-action conjecture
proof_stages.summary: For a totally real field \(F\) and a Hilbert modular eigenform \(f\), the talk defines the unit/motivic space \(U_f\) attached to \(\operatorname{Ad}^0\rho_f\), decomposes it over \(\mathbb C\) using the real embeddings, and states the conjectural graded \(\overline{\mathbb Q}\)-action on coherent cohomology matching partial complex conjugations after scalar extension.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular surface and regulator matrix
proof_stages.summary: In the first nontrivial case \([F:\mathbb Q]=2\), the conjecture is made explicit in terms of the basis classes \(\omega_I\), units forming a \(2\times2\) regulator matrix, and the predicted algebraicity of inverse-regulator linear combinations, with the determinant case related to a rank-two Stark-type statement.

proof_stages.label: Doi-Naganuma base-change theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a Doi-Naganuma lift, i.e. the base change of a classical weight-one form to a real quadratic field, the regulator diagonalizes into two logarithms of Stark units, reducing the conjecture to two algebraicity assertions and yielding the stated theorem under technical level conditions and a non-\(A_5\) projective-image hypothesis.

proof_stages.label: Proof via swap involution, special cycles, and theta lifts
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses the algebraic involution swapping the two real embeddings, pulls cohomology classes back to special cycles on an orthogonal Shimura variety to compute the relevant scalar, and identifies that scalar as a ratio of Petersson norms through theta-lift identities for the Doi-Naganuma correspondence.

proof_stages.label: Relative quadratic-base generalization
proof_stages.summary: The final part sketches an expected extension from \(\mathbb Q\) to a totally real base field \(F_0\) with a quadratic totally real extension \(F/F_0\), predicting an analogous rational subspace and regulator description for base-changed forms.

examples.name: Real-coefficient weight-one eigenform
examples.note: Taking a form with real Fourier coefficients makes the contragredient form equal to the original form, so the Serre-duality pairing becomes the Petersson norm; this is a simplifying illustration of the rationality/Stark normalization.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to a weight-one eigenform with real coefficients, identifies its contragredient with itself, pairs the associated cohomology class with the form, and relates the resulting Petersson norm to a special L-value/logarithm of a unit

passes.reason: A real-coefficient weight-one eigenform is used as a simplifying representative case with f^c=f for the Petersson pairing, not as a failure case or classification exception.
weight one is a minimal/boundary weight for modular eigenforms, and the real-coefficient case is used only as a simplifying self-conjugate illustration, not as a refutation.
A real-coefficient weight-one eigenform is used as a simplifying self-conjugate case with f^c=f, not as a failure case or sporadic/extreme object.
A weight-one eigenform is a lowest-positive-weight/boundary case of modular eigenforms, and here the real coefficients are used only to simplify by making f^c=f, not as a counterexample.
A real-coefficient weight-one eigenform is a self-conjugate boundary/minimal-weight modular eigenform used to simplify the Petersson pairing, not to refute a claim.
A real-coefficient eigenform is used only as a simplifying self-conjugate case, with weight one being the minimal/boundary holomorphic weight rather than a sporadic or adversarial example.

A real-coefficient weight-one eigenform is used as a simplifying self-conjugate illustration for the Petersson pairing, not to refute a claim; weight one is a boundary/minimal-weight case for holomorphic modular forms with special Artin-representation behavior.

A weight-one eigenform with real coefficients is used only as a simplifying illustrative case where the contragredient equals the form, not to refute a claim, and it has no degenerate, extremal, exceptional, or maximally symmetric structure.
Chosen only to simplify the discussion by making the conjugate form satisfy f^c=f, not to refute a claim, and otherwise an ordinary member of a non-sporadic family.
used as a simplifying case where the contragredient form equals the form in the Petersson pairing, with no maximal symmetry, sporadic, extremal, or degenerate structure
It is used as a simplifying illustrative case with f=f^c for the Petersson pairing, and weight one is a lowest/boundary positive integral weight rather than an obstruction.
Used only as a simplifying self-conjugate modular eigenform; weight one is the minimal/boundary positive holomorphic weight, not a sporadic, degenerate, or maximally symmetric object.
